title: Arnold Ventures Parole Project
text:
The Council of State Governments (CSG) Justice Center intends to illuminate parole release policies and practices in the United States and how they impact prison population sizes and lengths of stay, using data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) from up to 45 states to:

Examine trends in their parole-eligible populations;
Conduct original empirical research on racial, ethnic, and gender disparities on the portion of sentences that are served in carceral settings past parole eligibility; and
Conduct deep dives into selected states to shed light on parole board decision-making. For these states, we will seek to identify any disparate decision-making and associated outcomes and recommend changes to policy and practice to prevent unnecessarily long prison stays while maintaining public safety.

title: Missing Data
text:
Questions

Depending on the state, can we assume that people in prison with missing parole eligibility data who have a sentence length of “Life, LWOP, Life plus additional years, Death” are not eligible for parole? Does offense type matter? For example, in the state of Georgia, 30% of people have missing parole eligibility data. Of these people, 70% had a violent offense and 20% had a Life/LWOP/Death sentence length. Of the people with a Life/LWOP/Death sentence length and missing parole eligibility data, 97% had a violent offense.

Depending on the state, can we assume that someone is not eligible for parole because they have a short sentence? Is time served so brief that the parole process might not be deemed necessary? For example, in Georgia, 83% of people with a sentence length of <1 year are missing parole eligibility information.

Do we need to take into account the prison sentencing system of the state to determine who is potentially eligible or not eligible for parole? For example, in Georgia, “While the grant of parole is discretionary, consideration for parole is often not; most inmates are entitled to automatic consideration for parole. In general, this occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence. However, at minimum, a person serving a misdemeanor sentence must serve 6 months and a person serving a felony sentence must serve 9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.”
 Word document in Sharepoint for discussing missingness.


Parole Eligibility Status by Sentence Length
Data: NCRP year-end population

title: National Trends
text:
With the support of Arnold Ventures, the Council of State Governments (CSG) Justice Center analyzed data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) Prisoner Series, and Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice to gain deeper insights into the relationship between parole practices and prison populations. The study specifically focused on understanding the dynamics of parole eligibility and lengths of stay, with a specific emphasis on examining racial, ethnic, and gender disparities.

People in Prison Currently Eligible for Parole

These maps show the percentage and number of individuals in prison in each state during 2020 who, although eligible for parole, remained incarcerated. Toggle between viewing data based on the percentage of these individuals or the actual number of eligible people still in prison. The stark variations across states emphasize the diversity of parole policies and practices in the U.S.





Percentage of PeopleNumber of People

























Notes: NCRP Data is from 2020. The parole-eligible prison population only includes people with sentence lengths ranging between 1-25 years and people incarcerated for a new crime.










Parole Eligibility Data by State


The table provides a comprehensive overview of state-by-state data on the number of people in prison and their parole eligibility status, including the percentage of individuals currently eligible for parole, those who will be eligible in the next 1-5 years, and the proportion for which parole eligibility data is missing or low, due to the abolition of parole or lack of data in certain states.

title: Georgia

Prison PopulationParole EligibilityReleasesDisparities








Admission Types


Prison populations are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns/revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.
In Georgia in 2020, people in prison for new court commitments accounted for 91% of the prison population, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 9%.




















Prison Population Trends

The graph below provides a decade-long perspective on the prison system in Georgia, highlighting three key metrics from 2010 to 2020. It showcases the overall prison population trend, comparing it with the number of individuals who were released from prison to parole, as well as those who met the eligibility criteria for parole but remained incarcerated.














Who's in Prison?





Race and EthinicityGenderAge







In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.















In 2020, male individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.















In 2020, individuals aged 25-34 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for new court commitments, while individuals aged 35-44 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for parole returns and revocations.
















Sentence Length



















Criminal Offense






















How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

While the grant of parole is discretionary, consideration for parole is often not; most inmates are entitled to automatic consideration for parole. In general, this occurs after serving 1/3 of the total sentence. However, at minimum, a person serving a misdemeanor sentence must serve 6 months and a person serving a felony sentence must serve 9 months. Inmates serving 21 years or more, but not a life sentence, become eligible after serving a 7 year term. In addition, inmates convicted of certain serious crimes (e.g. aggravated assault, incest, drug trafficking) are not eligible for parole until they have served 7 years or 1/3 of the prison term, whichever comes first.








Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 11,827 people were eligible for parole but still in prison for new crimes and with original sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years. This group made up 25% of the prison population.












People who are Eligible for Parole





Race and EthnicityGenderAge







In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated, comprising 56% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.
















In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated, comprising 93% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.
















In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated, comprising 39% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.

















Sentence Lengths

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated with an original sentence length of 10-24.9 years, accounting for 66% of this group.















Criminal Offenses

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated for offenses related to aggravated or simple assault, accounting for 26% of this group.













Missing Data

This section will have information about missing parole eligibility data for the state.

The chart below shows an annual breakdown from 2010 to 2020 on the proportion of the parole-eligible population that was released each year compared to those who remained incarcerated despite their parole eligibility.

Race and Ethnicity

For every White person in prison, there are 3.1 Black, non-Hispanic people in prison.










Note: The Relative Rate Index (RRI) measures racial and ethnic disparities by comparing rates between groups, often using White individuals as the reference. The RRI provides insight into the degree of overrepresentation or underrepresentation, with an RRI greater than 1 indicating that a particular racial or ethnic group is disproportionately represented. Source: BJS Prisoners (2020), Census (2020)























Predicted Probabilities

Predicted probabilities: Controlling for offense and sentence length, predicted probabilities of release before/at eligibility date.  Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date. Similarly, 80% of women and 74% of men were released before/on parole eligibility date
